topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise calm rhythm of my evening. A profile picture I instantly recognized, a distorted, sneering grin I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles jump beneath my skin. *Him.* Mark. The boy who used to dismantle my confidence with casual cruelty in the seventh grade. A wave of pure, hot fury washed over me, making my palms sweat. I didn't even need to click on the request; the sheer audacity of it was enough to make my breath catch. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the small living room like a caged animal, my fists clenching and unclenching. Every instinct screamed at me to call him, to confront him, but something held my tongue, a cold, brittle resolve settling in my chest. I just stared at the screen, my knuckles white against the desk edge, a silent promise of retribution forming in the hollow of my throat.

[story 2]

I found myself staring at the phone screen for what felt like an eternity. The name flashed: Mark. My stomach twisted into a tight, unpleasant knot. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a flush spreading across my cheeks that had nothing to do with the temperature of the room. It was ridiculous. After all this time, after I’d built walls so high, so sturdy, how could some relic from my past just slide back through the gate? My hands started to tremble, a fine vibration running up my forearms. I paced the length of the kitchen, kicking at a loose floorboard, the sound sharp and angry. "No," I muttered, the word scraping out of my throat, low and ragged. I wanted to smash the device, to rip the memory of that boy out of my mind, but I kept my hands still, forcing myself to breathe slowly, though the air felt thick and suffocating. The sheer disrespect radiating from that digital invitation made my vision narrow.

[story 3]

The email arrived at 2 AM, a jarring intrusion into my sleep. My eyes snapped open, immediately locking onto the familiar, infuriating avatar. A slow, dangerous burn started in my chest, spreading outward like wildfire. I sat bolt upright in bed, the sheets tangling around my legs. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a frantic need to lash out. I didn't move for a full minute, just letting the raw, churning feeling inside me build. When I finally moved, it was with swift, jerky motion. I snatched the phone, my fingers gripping it so tightly the plastic creaked. I didn't respond. Instead, I walked to the window, staring out at the distant city lights, my posture rigid, every line of my body coiled tight with suppressed violence. The silence in the room was deafening, filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.
